data.description:
`rebar list --type=<t>` only matches a single type. Passing a comma-separated list (e.g. `--type=task,story`) returns `[]` (no matches) rather than the union of the two types.

Contrast with --status, --priority, --has-tag, and --without-tag, whose help text explicitly documents comma-separated OR semantics. --type silently does not, so a caller expecting OR semantics gets an empty result with no error — the most confusing failure mode.

Acceptance criteria:
- `rebar list --status=open --type=task,story` returns the union of open tasks and open stories (not []).
- Behavior matches the OR semantics already used by --status/--priority/--has-tag.
- --type help text documents the comma-separated OR behavior.
- Unit/interface test covers the multi-type case.

Repro:
  rebar list --status=open --type=task,story   # -> [] today; should be union
  rebar list --status=open --type=task          # -> N tasks
  rebar list --status=open --type=story          # -> M stories

Discovered while listing parentless open task/story tickets (had to query each type separately and merge with jq).
data.title: list --type does not accept a comma-separated list of types
